past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 5 years ago, well-controlled on amlodipine
Osteoporosis, diagnosed 3 years ago, on calcium and vitamin D supplementation
Mild chronic kidney disease stage 2, incidental finding 2 years ago, stable
Remote history of major depressive episode 8 years ago, treated with sertraline for 1 year, self-discontinued
family_history: Mother died at age 78, had progressive memory problems in her last 5 years, never formally diagnosed
Father died at age 82 of myocardial infarction, no known cognitive issues
Older sister, age 66, reports some forgetfulness but not evaluated
Maternal uncle had Parkinson's disease diagnosed in his 70s
No family history of seizures or stroke

social_history.alcohol: Rare, one glass of wine on special occasions
social_history.occupation: Retired middle school teacher, retired 2 years ago
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband in a single-story home
social_history.exercise: Walks 20-30 minutes daily, tai chi twice weekly

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person and place; hesitant on exact date, correctly states month and year but gives incorrect day of the week. Speech is fluent with normal rate and prosody but notable word-finding pauses, occasional circumlocutory language. Attention preserved on digit span forward (6 digits) but reduced on digit span backward (3 digits). MMSE score 24/30, losing points on orientation (2), delayed recall (2), and serial sevens (1). Unable to recall 3/3 objects at 5 minutes even with category cues. Clock drawing shows correct circle and numbers but hands placed incorrectly (10 past 11 drawn as both hands pointing to 10 and 11). No evidence of aphasia, apraxia of speech, or visual neglect.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, equal and reactive. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Visual fields full to confrontation. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. No facial asymmetry. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk and tone normal throughout. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. No drift on pronator testing. No tremor at rest or with posture. No myoclonus observed during the examination.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception in all extremities. No cortical sensory deficits.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric throughout. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No Hoffmann sign.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing normal bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements performed normally.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal stride length and base. Tandem gait performed without difficulty. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) score 20/30, with deficits in delayed recall (0/5), visuospatial/executive (2/5), and attention (4/6). Animal fluency: 11 in 60 seconds (borderline). Trail Making Test Part B: 185 seconds with 2 errors (impaired for age and education).

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive memory difficulties and episodes of disorientation over the past 12 months
patient.history_present_illness: Mrs. Chen Wei is a 62-year-old right-handed retired schoolteacher who presents to the neurology outpatient clinic accompanied by her husband for evaluation of progressive memory difficulties over the past 12 months. Her husband first noticed changes approximately one year ago when she began misplacing everyday items such as her keys and reading glasses with increasing frequency. Over the subsequent months, she started forgetting recent conversations, sometimes asking the same questions multiple times within an hour. Approximately six months ago, she had an episode of becoming disoriented while walking in their neighborhood of 20 years, briefly unable to find her way home from the local market, which alarmed both patient and family. She has had several instances of leaving the stove on after cooking and forgetting to take her medications unless reminded. Her husband reports she has become less engaged in social activities and recently withdrew from her weekly mahjong group, citing difficulty keeping track of the tiles and game strategy. She has also had intermittent episodes of dizziness described as lightheadedness, particularly with position changes, which she attributes to her blood pressure medication. Of note, she occasionally reports vivid, brief dream-like images when falling asleep, describing scenes of people she does not recognize appearing in her bedroom doorway, though she recognizes these as not real. Her husband notes that her alertness and engagement seem to fluctuate throughout the day, with periods of being quite sharp alternating with periods of seeming 'foggy' or staring into space, though these fluctuations have been mild. She denies any falls, gait difficulties, tremor, hallucinations during full wakefulness, or loss of consciousness. She has not experienced any sudden neurological deficits, headaches, seizures, or changes in personality. She reports her mood has been somewhat low given her frustration with the memory problems, though she denies hopelessness, anhedonia, or suicidal ideation. She continues to perform basic activities of daily living independently but requires increasing assistance with managing finances and scheduling appointments.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 8 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric but at the lower end of normal range, reactive to eye opening
background.anterior_activity: Low-to-medium amplitude beta activity, symmetric, slightly more prominent on the left
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep spindles and vertex waves observed during brief drowsiness

findings.location: Bilateral posterior regions (O1, O2, P3, P4)

findings.frequency: Occasional bursts of 5-6 Hz theta activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Mild diffuse slowing during hyperventilation, returning to baseline within 60 seconds post-HV. No epileptiform discharges provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Symmetric photic driving response at 8-14 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Mildly abnormal EEG for age. Borderline slowing of the posterior dominant rhythm at 8 Hz and intermittent left temporal theta slowing. No epileptiform discharges or periodic patterns identified. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Brief recording (30 minutes). Sleep stage limited to drowsiness only.

findings.size: Scheltens grade 2 bilaterally (left slightly greater than right)

signal_characteristics.T1: Reduced hippocampal volume with widened choroidal fissure and temporal horn
signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: No signal abnormality within hippocampal formations

signal_characteristics.T1: Subtle widening of cortical sulci in temporoparietal regions, mild for stated age

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Confluent periventricular hyperintensities extending into deep white matter, bilateral frontal and parietal

volumetrics.hippocampal_volume_left: 2.8 mL (5th percentile for age and sex)
volumetrics.hippocampal_volume_right: 3.0 mL (10th percentile for age and sex)
volumetrics.total_brain_volume: 1068 mL (25th percentile for age and sex)

additional_observations: No mass lesions or abnormal enhancement post-gadolinium
Normal flow voids in the major intracranial arteries
Mild mucosal thickening in the right maxillary sinus (incidental)
mri.impression: Bilateral medial temporal lobe atrophy (Scheltens grade 2, left > right) with subtle temporoparietal cortical volume loss, concerning for early neurodegenerative process. Moderate periventricular white matter disease (Fazekas 2) and a chronic left putaminal lacunar infarct suggest concurrent small vessel cerebrovascular disease. No acute pathology. Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: BUN 22 mg/dL (H), Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (H), Total Cholesterol 228 mg/dL (H), LDL 148 mg/dL (H).
abnormal_values_summary: BUN 22 mg/dL (mildly elevated, consistent with CKD 2)
Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (mildly elevated, consistent with CKD 2)

Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Delayed Recall
Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Recognition

abnormal_values_summary: Delayed recall severely impaired (RAVLT 2/15, Rey Figure 8/36)
Trail Making B significantly impaired (185 sec)
Mild anomia on Boston Naming Test (48/60)

signal_characteristics.uptake: Increased tracer retention in bilateral frontal, parietal, temporal, and posterior cingulate cortices
signal_characteristics.cerebellar_reference: Normal cerebellar uptake used as reference region

additional_observations: No focal areas of markedly asymmetric uptake
White matter uptake within normal limits for non-specific binding
output.impression: POSITIVE amyloid PET scan. The pattern of tracer retention involves frontal, parietal, temporal, and posterior cingulate cortices bilaterally, which is the typical distribution seen in Alzheimer's disease. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.location: Bilateral temporoparietal cortices and posterior cingulate gyrus

signal_characteristics.pattern: Bilateral temporoparietal and posterior cingulate hypometabolism
signal_characteristics.severity: Moderate reduction in FDG uptake (15-25% below age-matched controls)

output.impression: Frontal, occipital, sensorimotor cortices and basal ganglia show preserved metabolism. Clinical correlation recommended.

special_tests.Neurofilament light chain: 18.5 pg/mL (mildly elevated for age; reference <12 pg/mL)

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 14 cmH2O. WBC: 1 cell/µL (normal) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 38 mg/dL. Glucose: 62 mg/dL (ratio 0.66 (serum glucose 94 mg/dL)). Aβ42: 385 pg/mL (low; reference >500 pg/mL). Aβ42/40 ratio: 0.048 (low; reference >0.061). Total tau: 485 pg/mL (elevated; reference <400 pg/mL). Phospho-tau 181: 72 pg/mL (elevated; reference <60 pg/mL). 14-3-3 protein: Negative. RT-QuIC (prion): Negative. Neurofilament light chain: 18.5 pg/mL (mildly elevated for age; reference <12 pg/mL).

abnormal_values_summary: APOE ε3/ε4 genotype (heterozygous ε4 carrier, increased AD risk)

output.query: early Alzheimer's disease cognitive decline treatment cholinesterase inhibitors 2024

results.title: Lecanemab in Early Alzheimer's Disease - CLARITY AD 18-Month Extension
results.finding: Lecanemab continued to show reduced decline on CDR-SB vs placebo over 18 months of open-label extension. Greatest benefit in early-stage (MCI/mild AD) patients with confirmed amyloid pathology.

results.title: AAN Practice Guideline: Mild Cognitive Impairment Due to Alzheimer's Disease
results.finding: Recommends cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) as first-line symptomatic treatment. Memantine may be added at moderate stage. Emphasizes non-pharmacological interventions including cognitive stimulation, physical exercise, and caregiver support.

results.title: Aducanumab and Lecanemab: Disease-Modifying Anti-Amyloid Therapies for Alzheimer's Disease
results.finding: Anti-amyloid antibodies show modest but statistically significant slowing of cognitive decline in early AD. Require confirmation of amyloid pathology before initiation. ARIA (amyloid-related imaging abnormalities) is the main safety concern, occurring in 12-35% of patients.

output.summary: Current evidence supports cholinesterase inhibitors as first-line symptomatic treatment for early Alzheimer's disease. Disease-modifying anti-amyloid therapies (lecanemab) represent a new treatment paradigm for amyloid-confirmed early AD, though with modest effect sizes and risk of ARIA. Non-pharmacological interventions including cognitive stimulation, physical exercise, and social engagement remain important adjunctive strategies.

output.proposed: Donepezil 5 mg once daily at bedtime
interactions: Amlodipine: No significant interaction; may be co-administered safely
Aspirin: Minor interaction; both may increase GI bleeding risk, but clinical significance is low at aspirin 81 mg dose
Calcium carbonate: No clinically significant interaction
warnings: Cholinesterase inhibitors may cause bradycardia; monitor heart rate, especially given concurrent amlodipine (which can also slow HR)
GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea, vomiting) common at initiation; start at 5 mg and titrate to 10 mg after 4-6 weeks if tolerated
Vivid dreams and insomnia reported; evening dosing may mitigate, or switch to morning if sleep disruption occurs
Use caution in patients with CKD; mild CKD stage 2 does not require dose adjustment but monitor renal function
output.formulary_status: Preferred generic; available without prior authorization
alternatives: Rivastigmine patch 4.6 mg/24 hr (may have fewer GI side effects)
Galantamine ER 8 mg daily (alternative cholinesterase inhibitor)

additional_observations: No evidence of reduced dopamine transporter density in the striatum
output.impression: NORMAL DaTscan. Symmetric and preserved dopamine transporter uptake in bilateral caudate nuclei and putamina. Clinical correlation recommended.

differential.key_distinguishing: Hypnagogic hallucinations and cognitive fluctuations raise concern, but no parkinsonism, no REM sleep behavior disorder, and DaTscan would be normal; source case originally diagnosed as DLB but this patient lacks core DLB features

Periventricular white matter disease (Fazekas 2) and chronic lacunar infarct suggest vascular contribution; however, the predominantly amnestic profile with hippocampal atrophy pattern favors AD as the primary pathology
White matter disease and lacunar infarct present, but cognitive profile is amnestic rather than dysexecutive, and hippocampal atrophy disproportionate to vascular burden

History of prior depression and mildly elevated GDS, but cognitive deficits are progressive, involve storage-type memory failure, and structural neuroimaging shows atrophy inconsistent with pure depression

Subacute rapidly progressive dementia with periodic EEG discharges would raise concern in the source case context, but this patient has an insidious 12-month course without myoclonus or periodic discharges; CSF 14-3-3 and RT-QuIC negative

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with volumetrics to evaluate for structural causes and neurodegenerative atrophy patterns
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens grade 2) with temporoparietal volume loss and concurrent white matter disease

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory workup to rule out reversible causes of dementia (TSH, B12, folate, RPR, HIV, CBC, BMP)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal TSH, B12, RPR, HIV; mildly elevated BUN/Cr consistent with known CKD; elevated cholesterol as vascular risk factor

optimal_actions.action: Request formal neuropsychological testing to characterize cognitive domains affected
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Predominantly amnestic profile with severe delayed recall deficits, impaired recognition, reduced semantic fluency, and executive dysfunction

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate for subclinical seizure activity or periodic patterns
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Borderline slowed PDR and mild temporal slowing; no epileptiform discharges or periodic patterns

optimal_actions.action: Consider CSF biomarkers (Aβ42, phospho-tau, total tau) or amyloid PET for biomarker confirmation of AD pathology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Decreased Aβ42, elevated phospho-tau and total tau consistent with AD; negative 14-3-3 excludes CJD

optimal_actions.action: Consider APOE genotyping and early-onset AD gene panel given age <65
optimal_actions.expected_finding: APOE ε3/ε4 heterozygote; no pathogenic APP, PSEN1, or PSEN2 variants

optimal_actions.action: Search medical literature on current treatment options for early Alzheimer's disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Cholinesterase inhibitors as first-line; anti-amyloid therapies for confirmed amyloid pathology

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions before initiating cholinesterase inhibitor therapy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Donepezil safe with current medications; monitor for bradycardia and GI side effects

critical_actions: Order brain MRI to evaluate atrophy pattern and exclude structural lesions
Obtain labs to rule out reversible causes of dementia (TSH, B12, RPR at minimum)
Perform or request formal neuropsychological testing to characterize cognitive profile
Obtain AD biomarkers (CSF or amyloid PET) to confirm amyloid pathology given early-onset presentation
Assess for depression as a contributing factor given history and mild depressive symptoms
Consider genetic testing given early onset (<65 years)
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease based solely on cognitive decline without rapidly progressive course or supportive biomarkers
Attributing all cognitive symptoms to depression without objective neuropsychological and biomarker evaluation
Starting antipsychotic medications for hypnagogic hallucinations (normal phenomena not requiring treatment)
Initiating memantine monotherapy in early-stage AD (not indicated as first-line at this stage)
Ordering unnecessary invasive procedures without first completing standard non-invasive workup
key_reasoning_points: The insidious 12-month progressive amnestic syndrome in a 62-year-old with MMSE 24 and MoCA 20 is the hallmark presentation of early Alzheimer's disease

Hypnagogic hallucinations (vivid images when falling asleep) are a normal phenomenon and should not be confused with the spontaneous, fully-formed visual hallucinations of DLB that occur during full wakefulness
Mild cognitive fluctuations can occur in many conditions including early AD and do not by themselves suggest DLB without other core features (parkinsonism, REM sleep behavior disorder)
The source case (DLB with PSDs on EEG) differs from this presentation because this patient lacks true visual hallucinations during wakefulness, lacks parkinsonism, and has a more gradual course
Bilateral hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens grade 2) with temporoparietal volume loss is the characteristic MRI pattern of early AD
The presence of Fazekas grade 2 white matter disease and a chronic lacunar infarct introduces a vascular confound that must be considered (mixed dementia vs pure AD)

The neuropsychological profile showing storage-type memory deficit (failed recall AND recognition) distinguishes AD from subcortical/vascular cognitive impairment where retrieval is primarily affected
CSF biomarkers (low Aβ42, elevated p-tau and t-tau) provide biochemical confirmation of AD pathology, and negative 14-3-3/RT-QuIC exclude prion disease
APOE ε4 carrier status increases AD risk but is not diagnostic by itself; it supports the diagnosis in the context of consistent clinical and biomarker findings
Low-normal B12 (310 pg/mL) is a potential confounder that should be evaluated further with methylmalonic acid but is unlikely to explain the full clinical picture

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate difficulty due to: (1) concurrent vascular findings on MRI (Fazekas 2 white matter disease, lacunar infarct) creating diagnostic uncertainty between pure AD and mixed dementia; (2) hypnagogic hallucinations and mild cognitive fluctuations that could raise a red herring consideration of DLB; (3) history of depression as alternative explanation for cognitive symptoms; (4) early onset age (62) requiring consideration of genetic causes; (5) source case was actually DLB, adding clinical complexity to the differential reasoning
